constraints.text: Additional safety concerns include physical and biological hazards such as harmful microorganisms.

steps.text: Address logistical constraints and requirements for the sampling effort, including sample holding times, technique selection, representative sampling, and cost restrictions.

Consider additional characteristics when selecting sampling equipment and techniques, such as material/chemical compatibility, sample volume, physical requirements, ease of operation, convenience, decontamination effort, field processing, and optimized techniques.

Evaluate possible required performance characteristics for the sampling technique, including selectivity, microbial viability, storage/preservation, area/volume sampled, detection/quantitation limits, robustness, ruggedness, resource requirements, safety, waste minimization, operational limits, and QA/QC.
Optimize each significant parameter serially.
Test previously optimized parameters to ensure they remain optimal.
Make necessary adjustments to previously optimized parameters.
Combine multiple parameters to form a Latin square-type design for simultaneous optimization.

preconditions.text: The analytical method must be constant.

constraints.text: Optimization should be weighted toward parameters that represent significant programmatic concerns rather than those that are easiest to measure.
Wetted-wall cyclonic sampler may malfunction at temperatures below freezing.
Semisolid media are subject to dehydration, depending on atmospheric conditions, which may limit the duration of sampling time.
Holding time and temperature can affect microbiological samples, potentially causing either an artificial increase or decrease in the number and viability of target organisms.
Field conditions such as the requirement to wear protective gear may limit the performance of a technique.

steps.text: Include known operational limits in technique documentation.
Describe testing conditions in technique documentation so that potential users can assess whether test conditions accurately replicate potential difficulties.

Incorporate all technique performance components (including personnel, laboratory facilities, reagents, supplies, equipment, calibrations, reporting standards, record keeping, data analysis, safety, and quality) into the sampling technique development.

preconditions.text: Ensure an appropriate analytical assay or set of assays is available to measure and interpret the performance measures of sampling techniques.

constraints.text: Field conditions such as meteorological conditions, low light levels, or the absence of horizontal surfaces may limit the performance of a technique.
The requirement to wear protective gear may limit the performance of a technique.
Decisions on incorporating performance factors must be based on their impact on the technique used, data quality, and whether the data are acceptable for use.
Material Safety Data Sheets on file and readily available
Biosafety in Microbiological and Biomedical Laboratories (BMBL) for biosafety
All hazards associated with collecting a sample, including physical and environmental dangers (e.g., inclement weather, slip hazards, unsafe atmospheric conditions, shock hazards)
All hazards associated with packing and shipping samples and wastes, including ensuring that shipping containers are free from contamination
Medical clearances and appropriate vaccinations
Disinfection and clearance procedures required for specific microbes
Emergency procedures, incident reporting and recordkeeping
Department of Transportation (DOT), Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), International Air Transport Association (IATA), state and other hazardous material shipping regulations
Locations and contact information for local hospitals, and emergency contacts for personnel
Locations and availability of emergency showers, eyewash stations and other first aid

steps.text: Develop a security plan that includes the site, staff, storage, and transport of potentially hazardous materials.
Contact state or local regulatory agencies early to determine state or local requirements and available treatment and disposal options.
Document the management system in the laboratory's quality manual and other referenced quality documents.
Begin sampling or analytical work.

preconditions.text: The corresponding health and safety plan must be approved by the local Safety, Health and Environmental Management (SHEM) office or the chemical hygiene officer.
A management system must be in place that accurately reflects the operating and QA/QC programs in the laboratory.

steps.text: Document training in laboratory records, including a description of the training program content and duration.
Maintain training records and performance evaluation records so they are readily available.
Review SOPs annually.
Modify SOPs as necessary based on the annual review.
Maintain proper and adequate records and files, including sample tracking, analytical data, reports, and QC data.
Electronically scan or otherwise convert hard copy records to an electronic format.
Back up electronic records regularly.
Store records securely to ensure they can be retrieved easily.
Design the sampling plan.

constraints.text: Ensure that collected samples properly represent the specific environment that is being sampled.
Ensure QC in the field and laboratory by using blanks and duplicate, replicate, or spiked samples.

steps.text: Complete field sampling forms (paper or electronic) with all relevant sample information at the time of sampling.
Perform field measurements (such as pH and temperature) on a separate subsample.
Discard the separate subsample used for field measurements.

constraints.text: Do not prepare a conductivity measurement with a sample that previously was used to measure pH, as potassium chloride from the pH probe may affect the conductivity reading.
Perform field measurements on a separate subsample to avoid contaminating samples for laboratory analysis.

steps.text: Check chemical preservatives for efficacy.
Ensure sample bottles contain sufficient sodium thiosulfate to neutralize residual chlorine.

Evaluate sample integrity elements, including temperature control, storage times and conditions, preservation chemicals, container compatibility, labeling/seal integrity, volume, and contamination control.
Verify sample integrity using appropriate QC steps.

preconditions.text: Samples are collected from chlorinated water sources and the target analyte is a viable organism.

constraints.text: Samplers and analysts must be vigilant in preventing contamination of samples and reagents.

steps.text: Ship suspected contaminated samples or wastes as dangerous goods or select agents.
Package, label, and ship potentially hazardous materials according to ground or air regulations.
Maintain sample and packaging integrity to ensure safe shipment.
Store samples or wastes in a secure area protected from vermin and adverse weather conditions.
Separate hazardous and nonhazardous samples and wastes.
Prepare a written description of the technique.
Include numerical and descriptive specifications of the technique's operational limits and performance attributes in the description.

constraints.text: Organizational policy should be followed with regard to sample shipments.
Personnel with approved DOT/IATA training must perform all shipments of potentially hazardous materials.
Shipments may be subject to the Select Agent Rules (42 CFR 72.6).

steps.text: Document successful completion of validation and field verification studies in the sampling technique validation report.
Prepare a suitable report for placing in the public docket.
Provide the SOP or a thorough description of the sampling technique that is being validated.
Provide an overall summary of the validation report.
Provide background information on the sampling technique development.
State the purpose of the technique, including the measurement objectives and the intended use of the data.
Describe the experimental design for validating the sampling technique, including the test method/procedure and details of equipment/locations used with calibration status.
Describe the technique's scope and applicability.

preconditions.text: Validation and field verification studies are successfully completed.

constraints.text: At a minimum, the sampling technique validation report must address the information contained in Table 1.

steps.text: Peer review EPA sampling techniques according to the current version of the EPA Science Policy Council’s Peer Review Handbook.

preconditions.text: Prior to publication of EPA sampling techniques
